item.title: Energy minister urges calm over fuel stocks as consumer watchdog scrutinises price increases
item.description: The government has assured Australians that there is no fuel supply shortage, as the New South Wales government convenes a fuel security roundtable.

item.title: Punters 'unlikely' to see millions in ticket sales refunded as long-running festival shutters
item.description: Bluesfest's founder said it was "unlikely" festivalgoers, some of whom spent thousands on tickets and accommodation, would see any refunds.

item.title: Max lives in a 'renters' utopia', but some say the city's famous housing system isn't fair
item.description: As rental affordability hits a new low in Australia, a European city boasts a decades-long social housing model which houses about 50 per cent of its population.

item.title: For 200 years, this community has called an Australian island home. Now its future is in doubt
item.description: In one of Australia's most remote places, a unique community navigates rising seas and fears of relocation and cultural loss.

item.title: Fifth member of Iranian football team leaves Australia after withdrawing asylum bid
item.description: Team captain Zahra Ghanbari has withdrawn her asylum bid, after three other members of the team left Australia over the weekend.

item.title: Frozen in time: the legacy of the people of Pompeii
item.description: A new permanent exhibition on the ancient Roman city of Pompeii has showcased an arresting image of the moment when a catastrophic volcano eruption destroyed the city in 79 AD. Over 20 casts of bodies of residents in the city will be displayed at the exhibition, along with other findings such as plants and food that remained buried for centuries under metres of ash and lava.

item.title: Sentimental Value just won an Oscar. Meet the skateboard-smuggling director behind it
item.description: Joachim Trier reflects on his family, punk roots, messy lives and the quiet moments that inspire his films.

item.title: Iran warns countries not to get involved in war | Easy English 16 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Fresh warning over risky financial advice targeting young Australians
item.description: A survey commissioned by the country's financial watchdog suggests nearly two thirds of gen Z trust financial information on social media.

item.title: How to make SBS News your preferred source on Google
item.description: Google now allows you to pick your preferred sources in Top Stories.

item.title: Questions raised over Australia's long-term energy security
item.description: The Federal Government has authorised a temporary 60-day easing of sulphur standards to inject high-sulphur petrol into regional communities facing critical fuel shortages. While Energy Minister Chris Bowen maintains that national reserves are stable, critics and analysts warn that Australia’s decade-long failure to meet international stockpile obligations leaves the country vulnerable to prolonged geopolitical shocks.

item.title: Does a 'Great Firewall' loom? Why Russians are buying up walkie-talkies and road maps
item.description: Some believe Russia is testing a system that could cut it off from the global internet.

item.title: 'One Battle After Another' also wins Oscars battle
item.description: One Battle After Another has bested Sinners at this year's Oscars, winning six awards to Sinners' four. KPop Demon Hunters and Frankenstein were among the other winners at Hollywood's night of nights. Michael B. Jordan and Jessie Buckley won the top acting gongs.

item.title: Markets brace for rate rise and can you trust AI for financial advice?
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Mathan Somasundaram from Deep Data Analytics and looks ahead to tomorrow's RBA interest rate decision on what will be a big week for global central banks; plus Stephanie Youssef looks at how Generation Z is seeking out financial advice with ASIC's Adam Kirkland.

item.title: Government defends decision on Iranian women’s football team visas
Justin says he was a jealous boyfriend. Then he found a 'long-term fix'
item.description: "Constant reassurance" was a short-term fix for Justin's jealousy. It took therapy, time and meditation to find the "long-term" one.

item.title: The RBA faces its 'most complicated' rates call in years. What could it mean for you?
item.description: The Reserve Bank was until recently expected to keep rates on hold at today's meeting. Now economists aren't so sure.

item.title: Israeli ground offensive in Lebanon 'must be averted', countries say in joint statement
item.description: Leaders of Canada, France, Germany, Italy and the UK have warned of "devastating consequences" as Israel says it's conducting a ground operation.

item.title: 'We don't want to lose our town': Woolworths' move strikes fear among residents
item.description: The closure of Rio Tinto's bauxite mine will be tough enough for this Arnhem Land town, but now Woolworths may pull out too.

item.title: Changes to migration agent rules | SBS News in Easy English 17 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Australians in Lebanon face $17k travel nightmare as daughter needs medical care
item.description: The family had planned to come back to Australia at the end of April, but are now trying to return more urgently.

item.title: Has Vienna solved the problem on housing affordability?
item.description: Rental affordability has hit a new low in Australia - but a European city boasts a century-old social housing model, which houses about 50 per cent of its population. With low rental costs and rolling leases, it's led some to refer to it as a 'renter's utopia'.

item.title: Despite a rate rise, Australia's CPI will likely remain among highest in the world
item.description: Australia's consumer price index of 3.8 per cent is one of the highest in the world.

item.title: Interest rates go up: decision reflects growing uncertainty in the global economy
item.description: The Reserve Bank has hiked interest rates by 25 basis points to 4.1 per cent. It's the second rise in a row - and there could be more to come.

item.title: Matildas to take on nine-time Asian Cup winners China, with a familiar face on the team
item.description: Matildas head coach Joe Montemurro says tonight's game will be a "tactical battle".

item.title: RBA raises interest rates in March for second time in 2026
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Nick Stenner from Bank of America; Alice Shen from VanEck; and Sally Tindall from Canstar to find out why the RBA lifted interest rates, how the market reacted and what it means for borrowers.

item.title: The 90-day question looming over Australia's fuel price pain
item.description: Global oil disruption has put Australia's fuel security back in focus.

item.title: 'Cashing in big time': Who's profiting from the war in the Middle East?
item.description: War in the Middle East has left hundreds of people dead and left destruction in its wake — and there's also an "uncomfortable truth".

item.title: Trump says he doesn't 'need or desire' Australia's help in outburst over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Trump said his NATO allies were making a "very foolish mistake" and lumped Australia in on the list of countries he's unhappy with.

item.title: UN rights office says Israeli strikes on Lebanese civilians may be war crimes
item.description: The UN human rights office has called for an investigation into deadly strikes on displaced people sheltering in tents.

item.title: Zelenskyy, Starmer urge continuing support for Ukraine as world's attention turns to Iran
item.description: The war in Ukraine is intersecting with rising instability in the Middle East and pressure on global energy supplies. President Volodymyr Zelenskyy is in London, warning modern warfare is rapidly evolving and pushing for deeper defence cooperation. At the same time, the EU is moving to resolve an oil dispute threatening support for Kyiv.

item.title: SBS News In Easy English 18 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5 minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

item.title: Four-day week, jackets off: The world starts cutting back on fuel amid supply concerns
item.description: As parts of Asia adopt fuel rationing measures, the Australian government insists it's not on the cards.

item.title: 'A real crossroads': Donald Trump, his 'great test', and the fine line walked by US allies
item.description: Trump's latest outburst exposes the tightrope American allies are balancing on amid the war in the Middle East.

item.title: New mining CEOs, plus markets brace for US rates decision
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Jamie Hannah from VanEck about the day's market action including the latest global interest rate expectations and what it means for investments, while BHP announces who will replace CEO Mike Henry.

item.title: Government modelling 'more drastic' fuel scenario, Chalmers says, as war rages on
item.description: Under one scenario, oil would reach US$120 a barrel and take three years to return to its pre-war price.

item.title: I was on reality tv and lead a high-flying lifestyle. People envy me — I don't blame them
item.description: My life is centred around luxury — and I essentially drink for a living, writes former Real Housewives star Kyla Kirkpatrick.

item.title: How do Australia's petrol prices compare globally?
item.description: Australia's petrol prices have surged in recent weeks, but remain lower than in many parts of the world.

item.title: 'I couldn't sleep last night': Locals brace as Cyclone Narelle barrels towards Australia
item.description: The category five storm is expected to make landfall on Queensland's northern coast on Friday morning.

item.title: PM announces new fuel supply taskforce as price-gouging investigation launched
item.description: Anthony Albanese said Australia's fuel supply "is currently secure" but he wants the country to be "over-prepared".

item.title: Scottish MPs reject assisted dying - and England's House of Lords looks set to follow suit
item.description: A WARNING THAT THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS: Scotland's Parliament has rejected legislation that would have made Scotland the first part of the United Kingdom to allow terminally ill adults to end their lives. Members of the Edinburgh-based legislature voted 69 to 57 against a bill that would have let people in Scotland with six months or less to live seek help to end their life. The Scottish vote is part of a wider debate across the UK.

item.title: UN warn of growing landmine contamination as countries abandon treaty
item.description: The heads of the UN land mine action program for Ethiopia and Sudan have called for more funding and awareness over the munitions, which kill and maim a disproportionate number of children. They are launching the International Campaign to Ban Landmines, hosted by the United Nations Institute for Disarmament Research.

item.title: Emergency fuel reserves expected to begin flowing within days | SBS News in Easy English 19 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Dire Straits for Trump, Beijing takes a backseat and Cuba in the dark. Plus, the goodie bag bonanza...
item.description: The war in the Middle East is entering a dangerous new phase, with a global oil chokepoint at the centre of rising tensions, but just how far will NATO allies go to support Trump’s push to reopen the Strait of Hormuz? Plus, a delayed China meeting raises fresh diplomatic concerns, and why not everyone is feeling the economic squeeze.

item.title: Power bills in some states could drop by up to 10 per cent
item.description: Under proposed safety net prices, Australian businesses and households may see "welcome relief", according to the regulator.

item.title: 'Not surprising at all': Israel's tactics in Lebanon and a threat to 'finish the job'
item.description: Israel is conducting heavy airstrikes and a ground operation in Lebanon, killing hundreds and displacing nearly a million people.

item.title: $50bn wiped from ASX as Middle-East war intensifies and jobless rate rises to 4.3%
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Faraz Syed from Citi to find out why the RBA will look through the rise in Australia's unemployment rate at its next interest rate meeting; and Henry Jennings from Marcus Today details the day's market moves as oil surges more than 10 per cent overnight.

item.title: 'Sells false hope': MP scolds migration lawyers over humanitarian cases
item.description: Labor MP Julian Hill said some of the advocacy he had seen from migration attorneys "borders on unconscionable conduct".

item.title: Could Donald Trump be about to make a 'big mistake' in Cuba?
item.description: The US president has called Cuba a "failed nation" after the island recorded a 29-hour blackout this week.

item.title: What the Matildas are 'looking to improve on' against 'technical' Asian Cup final foes
item.description: Star defender Steph Catley says it's bound to be a "great game" on Saturday night.

item.title: 'Do not get more powerful than this': Cyclone Narelle set to slam far north Queensland
item.description: Cyclone Narelle has been downgraded to a category 4 storm and is set to make landfall at 7am (AEST) in far north Queensland.

item.title: Mosque defends Albanese visit, as PM claims hecklers upset over hate group outlawing
item.description: Muslim leaders said the PM's visit allowed them to express community "alarm and concern" over Israeli strikes in the Middle East.

item.title: Iran minister says Australia 'on the wrong side'; criticises 'sham' photo with footballers
item.description: Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaell Baghaei said Australia 'forced' Iranian footballers to seek asylum.

item.title: Trump administration applies pressure over reporting on war in Middle East
item.description: As the war in the Middle East continues, the United States is fighting a battle on another front: information.

item.title: 'Nothing to celebrate': War and displacement eclipse Eid in Lebanon
item.description: Across the Middle East, the holy month of fasting is ending under a dark cloud.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 20 March 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Wong urges against escalation of war in call with US counterpart Marco Rubio
item.description: The foreign minister said she and the US secretary of state agreed international efforts are necessary to ensure the Strait of Hormuz is not "held hostage" by Iran.

item.title: Markets bet on 3 rate rises this year & how AI is changing Australia's Rich List
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Tony Sycamore from IG Austraila about the day's market action and why markets are now pricing in three rate rises this year; plus Stephanie Youssef looks though The Australian's Rich List 250 with list editor John Stensholt.

item.title: Election Day in South Australia: Everything you need to know
item.description: While Labor is expected to hold on to power, all eyes will be on the race — seen as a test case amid a surge in support for One Nation.

item.title: Australians urged to work from home and drive slower to save fuel
item.description: The recommendations from the world's top energy agency also include opting for public transport and reducing air travel.

item.title: 'Rapidly deteriorating': Abbas' 3am decision exposes tragic truth facing millions in Lebanon
item.description: Abbas Ayoub has fled Beirut's Dahiyeh for the third time, as more than a million Lebanese people are forced from their homes.

item.title: Looking at this content online could feel like someone's watching over your shoulder — or worse
item.description: Could new age verification requirements make adult consumers of porn concerned for their privacy?

item.title: COVID 2.0? Industry group demands fuel carve-out, warning new builds at risk
item.description: Higher fuel prices are already being felt across the construction industry, putting Australia's target of 1.2 million homes at risk.

item.title: INTERVIEW: SBS talks to Premier Peter Malinauskas as South Australia goes to the polls
item.description: South Australia votes to elect its 56th Parliament on Saturday, 21 March. YouGov’s final poll ahead of the election showed Labor is on track to secure its highest-ever two-party preferred vote in the state party's history, while the Liberals face their worst result to date. One Nation however is at 22 percent. SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson has spoken with South Australian Premier Peter Malinauskas about his hopes for the election - and what he thinks of Pauline Hanson.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Ahead of South Australia's state election, SBS talks to One Nation candidate Cory Bernardi
item.description: South Australians head to the polls on Saturday, 21 March, to elect the state’s 56th Parliament, with the latest YouGov poll pointing to a strong result for Labor. The survey suggests Labor could achieve its highest-ever two-party preferred vote in South Australia, while the Liberal Party is tracking toward a historically poor outcome. Meanwhile, Pauline Hanson’s One Nation is polling at 22 per cent, signalling a significant presence in the vote. SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson spoke with One Nation candidate and former Liberal Senator Cory Bernardi.

item.title: War casts a shadow over new year festivities for millions worldwide
item.description: Festivities marking the spring equinox are tinged with sorrow, as war continues to devastate the Middle East. Even so, more than 300 million people worldwide are expected to welcome Nowruz - the new year - with families, food and fresh hope.

item.title: Northern Territory residents batten down as cyclone expected to intensify over Gulf of Carpentaria
item.description: Despite a downgrade to a Category 2 system, Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to strengthen again.

item.title: Trump considering 'winding down' war in the Middle East, but won't agree to ceasefire
item.description: President Trump's comments come as the US military deploys thousands of further troops to the Middle East.

item.title: 'Grief and rage': This Nowruz, celebrations hide a 'devastating' reality for Iranian Australians
item.description: For many Iranian Australians, Nowruz this year is shadowed by devastation abroad. Yet rituals of family, food and unity remain a vital lifeline.

item.title: South Australian party leaders vote as state election draws a major national spotlight
item.description: All eyes are on the South Australian state election as Peter Malinauskas' Labor government contends with an ascendant One Nation.

item.title: 'A journey through ocean and fire': Australia remembers Rhoda Roberts' powerful work
item.description: Australians pay tribute to Aunty Rhoda Roberts, remembering her as a cultural champion.

item.title: Labor victorious as One Nation claw Liberal vote away
item.description: On Saturday night, Labor held 45.7 per cent of the vote, with the Liberals on 15.6 per cent and One Nation sitting on 19.5 per cent.

item.title: Matildas fall as Japan claim Women's Asian Cup crown
item.description: Australia has lost to Japan, who beat the Matildas 1-0 in the AFC Women's Asian Cup 2026 final.

item.title: 'There is a risk': Concerns over the new Iranian leader owning assets in Australia
item.description: Multiple reports show Iran's new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, owns a multi-million-dollar portfolio of luxury assets worldwide.

item.title: Becoming a 'slay queen' gave Habiba a life of luxury, but her lifestyle can come with a cost
item.description: In South Africa, women who profit from relationships with rich men are known as 'slay queens'. But there can be devastating consequences.

item.title: Rhoda Roberts AO: The cultural warrior who changed the nation
item.description: Tributes are being made to SBS Elder-in-residence and Widjabul Wia-bal woman, Auntie Rhoda Roberts, who has died at the age of 66. Her short battle with a very rare type of ovarian cancer ended on Saturday afternoon on her country surrounded by family. The decorated writer, actor and director was awarded an Order of Australia in 2016 for her service to performing arts and immense impact on and off the stage and screen. Rhoda Robert’s family has granted permission for SBS to broadcast images and audio, to honour and celebrate her life and enduring legacy.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Squeezing more kilometres out of your fuel tank
item.description: Fuel prices are climbing and tensions in the Middle East are putting even more pressure on what we pay at the pump. So how can everyday drivers squeeze more kilometres out of every tank? In this edition of Weekend One on One, SBS's Asha Abdi spoke to Andrea Matthews, Motoring Editor at RACV, about practical tips to help you stretch your fuel further without changing your life too dramatically.

item.title: Rain-battered communities face down second bout with slow-moving cyclone
item.description: Tropical Cyclone Narelle, which reintensified on Saturday, is expected to track across the Top End throughout Sunday and Monday.

item.title: New HIV drug arrives in Zimbabwe, promising protection but testing health systems after aid cuts
item.description: A new HIV drug has arrived in Zimbabwe, promising protection from the virus that has devastated the population in the past two decades. But there are concerns that testing health systems won't cope, after international aid cuts.

item.title: As the Matildas react to a tough Asian Cup loss, Montemurro feels this is just the beginning
item.description: The Matildas manager isn't convinced the team's golden age will end with its most senior players.

item.title: As the Matildas react to a tough Asian Cup loss, Montemurro feels this is just the beginning
item.description: The Matildas manager isn't convinced the team's golden age will end with its most senior players.

item.title: Japan defeats the Matildas in the Women's Asian Cup title final
'The nepo-tollah': Iran's new supreme leader may be tied to global multi-million dollar portfolio
item.description: As Iran is embroiled in an ongoing war and crippling economic conditions, multiple reports have shown its new supreme leader is linked to a multi-million dollar portfolio of assets all over the world. Some are concerned this money trail may reach as far as Australia.

item.title: Iran pledges to target vital Middle East infrastructure after Trump's Strait of Hormuz threat
item.description: Iran's military has said US energy and desalination facilities in the region would be targeted if Iranian power plants were struck.

item.title: Australia to face price 'bumps' but not fuel shortage despite oil tanker shipment cancellations
item.description: Six tankers scheduled to deliver oil to Australia have had their voyages cancelled as impacts from the war in the Middle East begin to bite.

item.title: 'A tough time': Australian Muslims celebrate Eid, 'worried' for family in the Middle East
item.description: As Muslims celebrate Eid, they are concerned about their family and loved ones in the Middle East.

item.title: Thumping victory for SA Labor as One Nation's vote surges
item.description: The Labor Party has comfortably won re-election in South Australia in a poll which saw support for One Nation climb to more than 21 per cent of the vote. Premier Peter Malinauskas secured a second term with a significantly increased majority while the Liberal Party secured around 19 per cent of the vote. As vote counting continues, it remains unclear whether One Nation can secure any lower house seats.

item.title: 'A party of disaffection': Does One Nation's historic surge in SA signal a broader trend?
item.description: One Nation's rise reflects voter frustration and isn't necessarily a long-term shift, one political expert has told SBS News.

item.title: Kyro wanted gender-affirming treatment but was told he had to wait to make the decision
item.description: Most young people in Australia need the consent of a guardian to access gender-affirming care. In some parts of the country, there are additional restrictions.

item.title: Far from the frontline, close to the fallout: How the Middle East war is testing Australia
item.description: Australia is far from the Middle East conflict — but not untouched by it. As risks ripple outwards, Canberra's role is quietly being tested.

item.title: Israel destroys infrastructure in Lebanon, raising concerns about 'immense' civilian harm
item.description: The destruction of bridges and homes on Sunday marks a significant escalation in Israel's military campaign in Lebanon.

item.title: Global wars driving national security fears among young Australians
item.description: A new report shows a dramatic rise in the number of young Australians worried about national security.

item.title: Amid new guidelines, a warning data centres could 'cut corners', threaten energy, water supply
item.description: Australia has the second-largest pipeline of data centre construction in the world, after the US.

item.title: Government says people can 'make the call' on work from home amid fuel supply concerns
item.description: The world's top energy agency has called for Australian businesses to allow employees to work from home amid concerns over fuel supply.

item.title: Thinking of buying an electric vehicle? Here's what to know before making the switch
item.description: Soaring petrol prices are prompting some Australians to consider buying an electric vehicle.

item.title: Ex tropical cyclone Narelle expected to strengthen again in WA | SBS News in Easy English 23 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Fighting over Strait of Hormuz ramps up as Israel expands ground operation in Lebanon
item.description: Missiles have struck Israel, as Israeli forces expand operations on the ground in Lebanon. The developments mark a significant widening of the war in the Middle East, raising fears of there being no end in sight. Meanwhile, the US is warning Iran about the risks involved, if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened.

item.title: Shinrin Yoku or forest bathing: the '80s Japanese practice that is more timely than ever
item.description: In a climate of increasing global conflict and uncertainty, people are adopting a peaceful practice to reduce stress. Forest bathing is giving nature-lovers respite from a chaotic and challenging news cycle.

item.title: Energy watchdog chief warns Australia, says oil shock worse than past crises combined
item.description: Asia is at the forefront of the energy crisis, the head of the International Energy Agency has told an Australian audience.

item.title: One Nation secures first ever lower house seat outside Queensland. What does this mean for the Coalition?
item.description: The South Australian election has triggered a federal political crisis, with One Nation displacing the Liberals as the primary alternative to Labor and securing its most significant electoral foothold in nearly 30 years. As the Coalition grapples with whether to pivot right to reclaim regional voters or overhaul its suburban appeal, the result has forced a panicked Parliament House to recognise the populist surge as a permanent disruption to the traditional political order.

item.title: UK PM says ambulances targeted in 'deeply shocking antisemitic arson attack'
item.description: Four ambulances belonging to a Jewish organisation were set afire in what police are treating as an antisemitic hate crime.

item.title: With a global boom in AI, does Australia need more data centres?
item.description: As the use of Artificial Intelligence surges around the world, the number of data centres required to power the technology is also growing rapidly, including in Australia. Now the Federal Government is laying out a plan aiming to ensure sustainability, as investment in data centres grows.

item.title: Fresh death cap mushroom warning for foragers as weather could lead to fungi flourishing
item.description: As mushrooms spring up across the country, Australians are being urged to only buy mushrooms from reputable markets and grocers.

item.title: Relief for drivers as rideshare giants raise prices amid fuel supply concerns
item.description: Uber and DiDi are among the businesses increasing their fees in response to the fuel supply issues triggered by the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Gold is down 20% from recent records, so what now?
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Daniel Martin from Alvia Asset Partners about the day's market action as the ASX200 edges closer to correction territory and finds out what the future holds for gold as global central banks reconsider monetary policy easing.

item.title: This fuel costs as little as 80c a litre in Australia. So why isn't everyone using it?
item.description: This fuel's slow demise came with the introduction of electric vehicles and the death of the local car manufacturing industry.

item.title: 'I literally have no choice': The workers bearing the brunt of Australia's fuel crisis
item.description: In response to fuel shortages, some governments recommend people work from home. But for those who can't, the bill is rising.

item.title: Iran denies Trump's claim of negotiations after US delays strikes on power plants
item.description: Donald Trump says there have been "very strong talks" between the US and Iran, but an Iranian official has described it as "fake news".

item.title: INTERVIEW: How much firepower does Iran have left?
item.description: The recent attempt by Iran to launch a missile attack on the UK-US air base on the island of Diego Garcia in the middle of the Indian Ocean has raised questions about the real strength of Iran's firepower. Iran previously has put a self-imposed limit on its ballistic missile program, limiting their range to (2,000 kilometers. Diego Garcia is well outside that range, 4000 kilometres from Iran. And as the war drags on, how much weaponry does Iran have in reserve? Justin Bronk, an airpower and defence analyst at defence think tank the Royal United Services Institute, has told the Associated Press Iran's resources are not limitless.

item.title: 'No-one is feeling it more': New measures to protect truckies from fuel price spikes
item.description: Under the amendment, truck drivers and road support businesses will be able to make an emergency application for a contract chain order.

item.title: Looming energy crisis has severe implications - and only one solution
item.description: The International Energy Agency is warning world leaders may not appreciate the depth of the energy crisis that is looming, as the US war with Iran develops. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has triggered a global fuel shortage experts say will have widespread and severe implications.

item.title: Coalition calls for stronger action as more petrol stations runout of fuel | Midday News Bulletin 24 March 2026
item.description: The coalition calls for additional action as more petrol stations run out of fuel; Iran denies US claims it's in talks to end the war in the Middle East; Cricket Australia still committed to the expansion of the Big Bash League.

item.title: Death toll rises to 66 after Colombian air force plane crash
item.description: Authorities are still investigating the cause of the crash.

item.title: How the war in the Middle East is triggering concerns over food security
item.description: The war in the Middle East, and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, has triggered what experts describe as one of the most significant energy crises in decades. But oil and gas are not the only vital resources that are impacted. The war has also severely disrupted the flow of fertiliser from the Middle East, triggering concerns over the impacts on local food production and food security.

item.title: Pause or pivot? Trump halts Iran strikes as talks disputed
item.description: Iran says no negotiations have been held with the United States, despite US President Donald Trump's claims US envoys have been holding talks with a respected Iranian leader. Mr Trump had earlier abruptly postponed plans to obliterate Iran's power plants. Meanwhile, Israel says it will continue to strike Iran and Lebanon, as the U-S considers a ceasefire.

item.title: Iran denies talking to US on deal to end war | SBS News in Easy English 24 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: More stations report running empty as fuel prices surge and tempers flare | Evening News Bulletin 24 March 2026
item.description: Australia and the E-U sign deals on trade - and defence cooperation; An Iranian group claims responsibility for an ambulance attack in London; Australia's Talia Gibson ends her impressive run at the Miami Open.

item.title: Cheaper EVs, easier job access: What's in the EU-Australia free trade deal for you?
item.description: Australia has cut tariffs on European goods and wound back a tax on electric vehicles, making their import cheaper.

item.title: After 14 years in visa limbo, Ferdos felt conflicted as Iran’s soccer team gained asylum
item.description: The 23-year-old said it was hard to understand how the government was able to act so swiftly, when "for us, it's been years and decades".

item.title: Call to end 'time-waste' appointments for struggling Aussies amid fuel costs-spike
item.description: Welfare groups are urging the federal government to assist unemployed Australians and boost support payments for those in remote areas.

item.title: Australia signs $10 billion trade and security pact with the European Union
item.description: Australia and the European Union have signed a landmark $10 billion trade and security pact to eliminate industrial tariffs and strengthen co-operation on cyber and maritime defence. While the Prime Minister has hailed it as an economic win, the deal has faced criticism from the National Party regarding agricultural access, and from the Greens over Australia's strategic dependency on Europe.

item.title: Victorian and Tasmanian teachers go on strike
item.description: Tens of thousands of education workers have walked off the job and marched on the Victorian parliament over a pay dispute. It's the first time in 13 years the state's teachers, principals and support staff have staged a 24-hour stoppage, severely disrupting lessons in hundreds of schools.

item.title: The consumer benefits of Australia-EU Free Trade Agreement
item.description: Australia’s new free trade deal with the European Union could unlock a $10 billion boost to trade, cut prices on European goods and expand access for local exports, though it comes with compromises for some producers. University of Technology Sydney Chief Economist Tim Harcourt explains what it means, before a look at the markets with Stuart Roberts from Stocks Down Under, as the ASX200 edges higher and mining stocks lift.

item.title: World gave Israel 'licence to torture Palestinians': UN rapporteur Francesca Albanese
item.description: Francesca Albanese, the UN's special rapporteur on the Palestinian territories, presented a report accusing Israel of torture.

item.title: Gary is part of the super-rich. He believes wealthy Australians should be paying more tax
item.description: Billionaires are multiplying fast — Australia’s count has doubled in a decade. Is it time to tax the super-rich? Even some of them think so.

item.title: The 'magic question': How long can Iran's choke on the Strait of Hormuz last?
item.description: Traffic through the strait is down 95 per cent from pre-war levels. Experts warn it can't return to normal quickly.

item.title: Iran appoints former Revolutionary Guards commander to key role | Morning News Bulletin 25 March 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Iran appoints a new security chief; Pakistan says it is ready to host talks between Iran and the US aimed at ending the war; and in sport, Australia's edition of the King's Baton officially unveiled ahead of the Commonwealth Games

item.title: What do we know about Trump's 15-point plan for Iran?
item.description: As Israel threatens to take control of southern Lebanon, the Trump administration sends Iran a 15-point plan for ending hostilities.

item.title: Russia launches deadly drone blitz across Ukraine in one of its biggest daytime attacks
item.description: Ukraine is reeling from one of the largest aerial assaults of the war, with nearly 1,000 drones launched in 24 hours.

item.title: Iran fired a missile at a remote Indian Ocean island. Now much of the world is worried
item.description: Iran fired two ballistic missiles at a remote island 4,000km away from its territory. How far can its weapons actually reach?

item.title: Australia’s housing targets hurt by Middle East war as targets slip nationwide
item.description: Australia is predicted to meet its national housing accord target of 1.2 million new homes by June 2030 — one year later than scheduled.

item.title: Bigger fines flagged for service stations and petrol suppliers who rip off motorists
Could First Nations wisdom and experience improve mental health and wellbeing?
item.description: The latest report on Closing the Gap Targets, released last month, found just four of the nineteen targets are on track. Among those targets Australia is failing to meet, the country is moving backward when it comes to reducing suicide rates for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people , which are now more than three times higher than for non-Indigenous Australians. First Nations health advocates and practitioners are urgently calling for investment in new approaches to preventative mental health led by First Nations wisdom and experience.

item.title: Teenage boy charged over alleged online extremist threats | Midday News Bulletin 25 March 2026
item.description: A Sydney teenager charged with terrorism offences; Donald Trump says Iran has given him a present, as he maintains talks to end the war are ongoing; Egypt international Mohamed Salah to leave the UK club of Liverpool.

item.title: Govt makes $100 million move on fuel 'cartel behaviour' but price gouging rules questioned
item.description: The consumer watchdog will be able to impose harsher penalties for suppliers found to be jacking up fuel prices.

item.title: Israel says it will seize southern Lebanon as far as Litani River
item.description: Israel says it will push up to 30 kilometres into southern Lebanon to establish a defensive buffer zone along the Litani River. Hezbollah is vowing to resist any advance and the United Nations warns the escalating conflict is deepening an already severe humanitarian crisis.

item.title: Record heat and extreme weather: Experts raise alarm on climate
item.description: The world has just experienced the hottest 11 years on record. A new climate report warns that the planet is not only warming, but storing more energy than it releases - accelerating climate change. But what is that likely to mean for us here in Australia?

item.title: Israel prepares to attack Hezbollah in southern Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 25 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: How much tax do Australians pay on fuel compared to other countries?
item.description: With fuel prices rising around the world, what portion is made up by tax?

item.title: Australia condemns Israel's plans for Lebanon campaign | Evening News Bulletin 25 March 2026
item.description: Australia warns Israel against the occupation of southern Lebanon; The government outlines where fuel is running out across Australia; The latest designs revealed for Brisbane's planned stadium for the 2032 Olympics.

item.title: 'Resilience, success and endurance': How to improve the refugee experience in Australia
item.description: A landmark conference about refugees in Australia is underway in Adelaide, aiming to bring refugees and multicultural communities together.

item.title: 'Alarming': Israel's proposed Lebanon seizure could take country back to 1980s, experts say
item.description: While "unsurprising", experts say Israel's decision to occupy southern Lebanon could be catastrophic.

item.title: Government's temporary ban on Iranian visitors begins, includes visas already granted
item.description: A new Arrival Control Determination will block Iranian passport holders from entering Australia on temporary visas.

item.title: Trade Minister Don Farrell defends beef quotas, name restrictions in EU-Australia trade deal
item.description: Trade Minister Don Farrell speaks to Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson. He is defending a new trade deal with the EU which will see beef exports to Europe increase eight-fold, despite the National Farmers' Federation calling for far higher quota. The government says the agreement is also a win for wine, seafood and other Australian producers.

item.title: ASX has best day in nearly a year as inflation eases ahead of the war
item.description: Australia’s inflation shows signs of stabilising but remains stubbornly above target, just as global tensions threaten to push prices higher for longer. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves breaks down the latest data from the Bureau of Statistics and what it means for interest rates with Westpac Senior Economist Pat Bustamante. Plus, the ASX200 delivers its strongest gain in nearly a year, driven by a surge in materials and retail stocks, while energy slips on shifting oil price expectations. Betashares’ Cameron Gleeson joins the podcast to unpack what’s moving markets and what investors are watching next.

item.title: Strikes near Iran, Israel nuclear sites risk 'catastrophe', UN rights chief warns
item.description: An Iranian strike recently struck the southern Israeli town of Dimona, home to a nuclear facility.

item.title: Could we be getting cheaper fuel? Everything you need to know about ethanol
item.description: The government is considering an increase in ethanol levels in fuel — but what could that mean cost-wise?

item.title: Fuel is just the start: The chain reaction hitting these parts of Australia
item.description: Some remote communities are facing impossible daily trade-offs.

item.title: In high school, I felt like I was lying to everyone. Then I hit my breaking point
item.description: Elliot was assigned female at birth but came out as trans at age 15. Before then, he felt like he was constantly in gender limbo.

item.title: Ukraine-Russia talks stall as attacks escalate | Morning News Bulletin 26 March 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Ukraine and Russia escalates their attacks, as talks continue to stall; Muslim groups condemn firebombing of a building set to become a Mosque; and in sport, Eight more former AFL players join landmark class action against the league

item.title: Renters continue to face a tight rental market and rising costs, according to new report
item.description: Australia's rental market remains tight, with low vacancy rates and rising rents, despite modest improvements.

item.title: Renters continue to face a tight rental market and rising costs, according to new report
item.description: Australia's rental market remains tight, with low vacancy rates and rising rents, despite modest improvements.

item.title: Fuel shortages set to drive up food prices across Australia
item.description: Rising fuel costs are beginning to filter through supply chains, with early signs supermarkets and transport operators are adjusting pricing.

item.title: INTERVIEW: National's Matt Canavan on the EU trade deal
item.description: Australia and the European Union have signed a landmark $10 billion trade and security pact to eliminate industrial tariffs and strengthen co-operation on cyber and maritime defence. The Prime Minister has hailed it as an economic win, but the deal has faced criticism from the National Party regarding agricultural access. SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson has been talking to National Party leader Matt Canavan about their objections to the deal and also about the Government's actions over the fuel crisis.]]

item.title: Cyclone Narelle expected to turn towards south
Iran reviewing US peace proposal, calls for Lebanon to be included in ceasefire
item.description: The White House has declined to disclose specifics of its proposal and threatened to escalate its strikes.

item.title: Meta and Google found liable in landmark social media addiction lawsuit
item.description: The verdict could mark a ‌turning point in the global backlash against their platforms' perceived mental health harms to youth.

item.title: Greens senator raises 'COVID-style' parliament amid fuel concerns
item.description: A Greens senator has urged his parliamentary colleagues to "scale back" and lead by example.

item.title: Groundbreaking lawsuit finds YouTube and Meta liable for harm to children | Midday News Bulletin 26 March 2026
item.description: A groundbreaking lawsuit finds Youtube and Meta liable for harm to children on social media; Australia and Germany announce strengthened security ties; South Australia takes on Victoria for the Sheffield Shield title.

item.title: US jury finds Meta and YouTube liable for harm to children in 'fight against big tech'
item.description: A US jury has found Meta and YouTube liable in a first-of-its-kind lawsuit that aimed to hold social media platforms responsible for harm to children using their services. The verdict could mark a turning point in the global backlash against their platforms' perceived mental health harms to kids and teens, more than two decades after the emergence of social media.

item.title: Australia shuts the door on Iranian visa holders | SBS News in Easy English 26 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: US signals conflict could end in weeks as war in the Middle East rages on
item.description: Missiles over Jerusalem, fires at a major Gulf airport, and warnings that one strike on a key Iranian island could trigger a wider war. As the US signals the conflict with Iran may end within weeks, the fighting itself is telling a different story.

item.title: Record wave of Russian drone attacks hits Ukraine
item.description: With peace talks stalled, Russia has launched one of its largest aerial attacks on Ukraine, killing civilians and damaging critical infrastructure-and bringing about Ukrainian retaliation.

item.title: Leigh Ryswyk becomes first AFL player to come out as gay
item.description: Ryswyk enjoyed a long career with North Adelaide after briefly playing for the Brisbane Lions in 2005.

item.title: What's a 'super' El Niño, and is Australia facing one?
item.description: Current forecasts suggest an El Niño could form later this year. If it does, it could bring a "new record hot year" in 2027.

item.title: Donald Trump and Iran continue to talk tough on discussions to end war | Evening News Bulletin 26 March 2026
item.description: Contradictory messages from the US and Iran over the war in the Middle East; Meta to appeal a finding it deliberately designed its platform to hook young users; The A-F-L unveils its new drug policy it says is the toughest in the nation.

item.title: Fuel shortages, price hikes drive rethink on ethanol
Warnings about possible unintended consequences of Iran visitor ban
item.description: Iranians holding visitor visas will be banned from entering Australia for six months starting from Thursday as migration law experts warn the ban could set a precedent for conflicts in the future.

item.title: Trump's mixed messages on the Middle East and Iran's 'nepo-tollah'
item.description: Donald Trump inflames confusion over war in the Middle East and where are Mojtaba Khamenei’s hidden millions? Plus, Israel raises annexation of southern Lebanon, Australia signs a massive trade deal with the EU, and some father-daughter time dictator style as North Korea’s Kim Jong Un rolls out the tanks.

item.title: 'A significant gamble': Is the US planning to seize Iran's 'forbidden island'?
item.description: Experts say Trump could use threats against Kharg Island as leverage in negotiations, though a ground incursion would be a "gamble" for him.

item.title: Iranian state media's Australian fuel reserves claim debunked
item.description: An Iranian state-aligned news outlet published an SBS News graphic that was edited to misrepresent Australia's fuel supply.

item.title: These powers have never been used. Could the fuel crisis force the government's hand?
item.description: The government faces growing calls to "pull the trigger" on laws that could control who gets fuel and where.

item.title: Government backs real wage increase to minimum wage
item.description: Australia’s minimum wage is back in focus, with the government signalling support for a real wage increase ahead of the Fair Work Commission’s June decision, as unions and business groups put forward competing claims. Independent economist Chris Richardson unpacks what a potential rise above inflation could mean for the economy. Meanwhile, the sharemarket dipped after a strong rally, with Darren Thompson from Equity Trustees breaking down the latest moves across sectors and what is driving investor sentiment.

item.title: Nicolas Maduro to appear in US court months after abduction
item.description: The hearing will likely see Maduro push for the dismissal of his case as lawyers tussle over who will pay the former leader's legal fees.

item.title: How do Australians really live? Our wrists reveal the answer
item.description: From who gets the most sleep to how often we move, here’s what a new study found.

item.title: The 'house of mum and dad': Boomers, young Australians divided over housing shift
item.description: A growing shift towards multi-generational living is emerging — but experts warn it’s no solution to Australia's housing woes.

item.title: G7 tells Israel, Hezbollah ‘sit down and negotiate’ | Morning News Bulletin 27 March 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Foreign Ministers meet to discuss conflicts at G7 talks in France; Donald Trump claims Iran has let tankers through the Strait of Hormuz; and in sport, The Melbourne Cricket Ground will host the first-ever regular season NFL game in Australia

item.title: INTERVIEW: How significant was the judgement against social media giants?
item.description: Experts say the lawsuit against Meta and YouTube is a first crack in the dam that could eventually lead to tighter regulation of social media platforms. On Wednesday, a California jury awarded millions of dollars in damages to a 20-year-old woman after deciding that Meta and YouTube designed their platforms to hook young users without concern for their well being. Snapchat parent company Snap Inc. and TikTok were also included in the lawsuit but settled for undisclosed sums before the trial. A day earlier, a New Mexico jury handed a $375 million penalty to Meta after determining the company knowingly harmed children’s mental health and concealed what it knew about child sexual exploitation on its platforms. In this episode of Weekend One on One we hear from Mark Stephens who's a specialist in international, appellate and complex litigation, and who works at the Howard Kennedy law firm.

item.title: Trump denies being 'desperate' for a deal, singles out Australia as 'not great' over Hormuz
item.description: Trump has insisted he's not looking for an exit ramp, as oil prices soar and pressure mounts to avoid a drawn-out war.

item.title: 'It's up to him': Albanese reacts to Trump's criticism, dismisses fuel excise cut call
item.description: The prime minister has tried to downplay fuel supply concerns, but acknowledged Australians are impacted by the war in the Middle East.

item.title: More fuel tankers en route to Australia as shortages spread | Midday News Bulletin 27 March 2026
item.description: Australia secures new fuel supply deals as shortages are felt across the nation; The government defends its moves in the Middle East, after criticism from Donald Trump; The International Olympics Committee rules on transgender athletes.

item.title: Nicólas Maduro fights US block on Venezuelan funds being used for legal fees
item.description: Captured Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro has asked the court to dismiss his case because he can't pay his legal fees.

item.title: Australia moves to reassure drivers of fresh supply as shortages spread
item.description: Australia's government insists it still has not received any direct requests from the United States for military aid in its war with Iran, after a public attack from President Donald Trump. The US president has criticised allied countries for not providing assistance in the conflict, as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz continues to put pressure on global oil prices. Meanwhile, the government maintains it's imperative for the war to be resolved as soon as possible, as the economic and social consequences unfold locally.

item.title: Europe to investigate Snapchat over underage protection concerns | SBS News in Easy English 27 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Lines drawn: The US extends pause on Iran's strikes, amid Middle East crisis
item.description: The US has maintained for days that negotiations between US and Iran are underway, a claim Tehran denies. From escalating military attacks to high stake warnings, the situation is shifting rapidly - with global implications at stake. As tensions mount in the Middle East, the US President says he's extending a pause on Iran's energy site strikes, for ten days.

item.title: Iran holds firm after a month of war — but what comes next is 'murky'
item.description: Four weeks in, the war is proving complex, with Iran's resilience reshaping both the conflict and its global impact.

item.title: Trump's signature to replace treasurer's on US paper currency
item.description: To mark the 250th anniversary of American independence, US paper currency will bear Donald Trump's signature, breaking a 165-year-old tradition.

item.title: Roofs torn off houses in WA as Tropical Cyclone Narelle makes landfall
The Coalition calls for halving of the fuel excise | Evening News Bulletin 27 March 2026
item.description: The Coalition calls for a halving of the fuel excise for three months; New Zealand outlines a four-phase plan for fuel shortages; Australia's Olympic team organisers welcome a ban on trans athletes in female competition.

item.title: EV loans double as motorists dodge the petrol pump
item.description: Unleaded petrol and diesel prices have soared in March after attacks on Iran prompted the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Heartbreak and hope as former winners face last chance to qualify for FIFA World Cup 2026
item.description: Only six opportunities for national teams to join the FIFA World Cup 2026 remain. Who will make it to the world stage in June?

item.title: What rising global fertiliser prices means for food costs and inflation
item.description: Markets held relatively steady to close the week, with the ASX200 dipping just 0.1 per cent but still breaking a three-week losing streak, as Adam Dawes from Shaw and Partners unpacks the key movers including sharp losses in tech and gains in energy stocks. At the same time, fresh warnings from the OECD point to rising global food prices if conflict in the Middle East continues to disrupt fertiliser shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. Stefan Vogel from Rabobank Research explains what that could mean for Australian farmers, supply chains and grocery bills in the month's ahead.

item.title: Western Australian residents forced to flee as cyclone brings destruction
item.description: The holiday town of Exmouth has already felt Tropical Cyclone Narelle's renewed force, with emergency services personnel expecting "significant damage."

item.title: Intensified tropical cyclone Narelle batters WA coast
item.description: There are fears that Tropical Cyclone Narelle could intensify to a Category 5 storm as it cuts a path down the Western Australian coastline.

item.title: Intensified tropical cyclone Narelle batters WA coast
item.description: There are fears that Tropical Cyclone Narelle could intensify to a Category 5 storm as it cuts a path down the Western Australian coastline.

item.title: Australia's biggest natural gas plants offline due to Cyclone Narelle
item.description: The outage comes at a time when Australia is already grappling with fuel shortages stemming from the war in the Middle East.

item.title: President Donald Trump says talks with Iran are continuing | Morning News Bulletin 28 March 2026
item.description: President Donald Trump says talks with Iran are continuing; A major Australian fuel supplier is calling for calm; and in football, The Socceroos defeat Cameroon.

item.title: Is the war in the Middle East is Australia's COVID 2.0 moment?
item.description: Some have compared the war in the Middle East's ramifications to the COVID-19 pandemic, but do they actually share similarities?

item.title: With many of its top figures dead, who is now running Iran?
item.description: Since its 1979 revolution, Iran has constructed a complex, layered power structure to ensure the survival of its theocratic system.

item.title: INTERVIEW: How do Australians view national security?
item.description: A new report shows a major rise in the number of young Australians worried about the country's preparedness in the face of crisis. Tim Wilford, who directed the research for ANU's National Security College, spoke with SBS News about what the results reveal about the concerns of voters.
